one_line_takeaway: By replacing PoisonedRAG-style trigger-concatenation with retriever-guided, MLM-based word-level substitution, RefineRAG reaches 90% ASR on NQ while cutting perplexity and grammar-error/repetition rates far below prior RAG poisoning attacks, demonstrating that stealthy, fluent poisoning texts can still reliably compromise black-box RAG systems.
key_finding: On the main comparison (Table 1), RefineRAG achieves the highest ASR of any method on both datasets -- NQ: L-ASR 0.90 / V-ASR 0.85 (vs. PoisonedRAG (W) 0.59/0.65, PoisonedRAG (B) 0.54/0.61, Prompt Injection 0.80/0.75, Corpus Poisoning 0.00/0.00); MSMARCO: L-ASR 0.83 / V-ASR 0.81 (vs. PoisonedRAG (W) 0.63/0.66, PoisonedRAG (B) 0.60/0.67, Prompt Injection 0.83/0.81, Corpus Poisoning 0.00/0.00) -- while simultaneously achieving the lowest stealthiness scores: PPL 118.33 (NQ) / 127.53 (MSMARCO), Repetition Rate 0.01 on both, and Grammar Errors 0.66 (NQ) / 0.86 (MSMARCO), versus PoisonedRAG (W)'s PPL of 372.93 (NQ) and GE of 6.54, and PoisonedRAG (B)/Prompt Injection's RR of 0.28/1.00 respectively. Retrieval F1 for RefineRAG (0.89 NQ / 0.70 MSMARCO) is slightly below PoisonedRAG (W) (0.95/0.66) and (B) (0.94/0.87) but still competitive, indicating the method trades a small amount of raw retrieval F1 for much better fluency/naturalness while retaining higher end-to-end ASR. Ablations (Table 4) show both stages are necessary: removing Stage I (No-I) drops NQ L-ASR from 0.90 to 0.72 and F1 from 0.89 to 0.63; removing Stage II (No-II) drops NQ F1 to 0.73. Table 2/3 show transferability: ASR ranges 0.81-0.92 across six victim LLMs (Llama2, Vicuna, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2.5, Qwen3-Max) on NQ, and attacks optimized on Contriever transfer to Contriever-msmarco and ANCE with ASR up to 0.70-0.83 (declining as retriever architecture diverges more, e.g. ANCE F1 drops to 0.63/0.47).
core_contribution: RefineRAG, a two-stage RAG knowledge-poisoning attack that reframes poisoning as a holistic word-level text-refinement problem rather than the 'Separate-and-Concatenate' (trigger + malicious payload) strategy used by prior work like PoisonedRAG. Stage I (Macro Generation) uses an LLM (DeepSeek-V3) in an exploration/exploitation loop, validated by a second LLM (Llama-7B) that discards any candidate failing to induce the target incorrect answer, to build a base of seed texts that are guaranteed to be semantically toxic. Stage II (Micro Refinement / Word-Level Optimization, WLO) POS-tags each seed to isolate content words (nouns/verbs/adjectives/adverbs, excluding question/answer keywords and stopwords), masks them one at a time, uses a BERT-Large MLM to propose up to K context-aware substitutes per masked word, and greedily/beam-searches (beam width B) for the substitution that maximizes embedding similarity to the target question as scored by a proxy retriever (Contriever), thereby raising retrieval rank while preserving fluency and low perplexity.
threat_model: Attacker goal: precision content manipulation (not general degradation) -- given a target question Q and a pre-defined plausible-but-incorrect answer Rt (generated by DeepSeek-V3 and manually verified to conflict with ground truth), mislead the RAG system into retrieving poisoned context and confidently outputting Rt. Attacker knowledge: proxy-assisted black-box -- no access to victim system internals, gradients, or configuration; the attacker uses a publicly available SOTA retriever (Contriever) as a local proxy to optimize texts, relying on a transferability assumption to affect unknown victim retrievers/LLMs. Attacker capability: confined strictly to injecting a small number of poisoning texts into the public corpus at inference/indexing time (e.g., editing public wikis, posting to indexed forums) -- five poisoned texts injected per target question in experiments; no access to training pipeline, model weights, or system code. Stated motivating scenario: high-stakes domains such as medical consultation or financial analysis.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Computational Overhead -- the iterative MLM-based Stage-II optimization is markedly more expensive than simple concatenation-based attacks; they suggest future distillation to accelerate it. (2) Dependency on Proxy Retrievers -- black-box transferability assumes embedding similarity between the Contriever proxy and the victim retriever; efficacy against structurally different (e.g., sparse) retrievers is untested and flagged as needing further study. (3) Defense Evasion Boundaries -- while RefineRAG evades fluency/perplexity- and repetition-based filters, its robustness against more advanced semantic defenses (e.g., external fact-checking) was not evaluated. Additionally (not stated by the authors but observable from the tables): retrieval F1 is sometimes lower than the white-box/black-box PoisonedRAG baselines, meaning RefineRAG is not uniformly best on every metric -- it is best on the combined effectiveness+stealthiness trade-off, and evaluation uses only two open-source 7B victim LLMs as the primary ASR targets (Table 2 extends to more models but only for NQ, not MSMARCO transferability across all six).

quantitative_results: RefineRAG NQ: F1 0.89, L-ASR 0.90, V-ASR 0.85, PPL 118.33, RR 0.01, GE 0.66, RL 0.55
RefineRAG MSMARCO: F1 0.70, L-ASR 0.83, V-ASR 0.81, PPL 127.53, RR 0.01, GE 0.86, RL 0.53
Ablation No-I (NQ): F1 0.63, L-ASR 0.72, V-ASR 0.69 (vs full model 0.89/0.90/0.85)
Transferability across 6 victim LLMs on NQ: ASR range 0.81-0.92 (Llama2 0.90, Vicuna 0.85, DeepSeek-R1 0.84, DeepSeek-V3 0.92, Qwen2.5 0.86, Qwen3 0.81)
Transferability across retrievers on NQ: Contriever F1 0.89/L-ASR 0.90, Contriever-ms F1 0.79/L-ASR 0.82, ANCE F1 0.63/L-ASR 0.70
Default hyperparameters selected via ablation: Stage I T=4 iterations, Stage II WLO=10 iterations, MLM candidates K=20, beam width B=3
